1: The superscription identifies this as David's victory song, paralleled in 2 Sam 22. אֶרְחָמְךָ יְהוָה חִזְקִי (ʾerḥāmĕkā YHWH ḥizqî, "I love you, LORD, my strength") — the verb rāḥam is from the root reḥem (womb): the tender, womb-like compassion a mother has for her child. Nowhere else in the Psalter is this verb used for the Psalmist's love toward God — it is normally used of God's compassion for humans (Psalms 103:13). The opening is uniquely intimate: David declares a tender, interior attachment rather than the more conventional honor-vocabulary.
2: Six metaphors accumulate without pause: סַלְעִי (salʿî, "my rock" — a cliff-face of limestone, the kind a fugitive hides in), מְצוּדָתִי (mĕṣûdātî, "my fortress/stronghold"), מְפַלְטִי (mĕphalṭî, "my rescuer/deliverer" — the one who causes to escape), מָגִנִּי (māginnî, "my shield" — the small personal shield), קֶרֶן-יִשְׁעִי (qeren yishʿî, "the horn of my salvation" — the raised animal horn as the symbol of lifted, victorious strength), and מִשְׂגַּבִּי (miśgabbî, "my high tower/stronghold" — a fortified high place). The accumulated images do not build logically; they accumulate intensively, each adding another dimension of divine protective capacity.
3: מְהֻלָּל אֶקְרָא יְהוָה (mĕhullāl ʾeqrāʾ YHWH, "I called to the LORD, who is worthy of all praise") — the participle mĕhullāl (pual of hālal: praised, worthy of praise) precedes the verb "I called." The worthiness of the LORD to be praised is stated as the presupposition of prayer: one calls on the one who deserves to be called on. The perfect of confidence follows: "and I am saved (wĕʾiwwāsheaʿ) from my enemies."
4: חֶבְלֵי-מָוֶת (ḥeblê-māwet, "cords of death") — ḥebel can mean either "cord/rope" or "birth-pang/anguish" (the same consonants, different vocalization). The context (wrapping around, entangling) points to the "cord" sense, though the birth-pang connotation adds a layer: the death-convulsions that seized him. נַחֲלֵי בְלִיַּעַל (naḥălê bĕliyaʿal, "floods of chaos/destruction") — bĕliyaʿal literally means "without profit/worth"; as a cosmic term it represents destructive, anti-order forces (2 Samuel 22:5 parallel: "floods of ungodliness"). The imagery merges physical danger with cosmic chaos.
5: חֶבְלֵי שְׁאוֹל (ḥeblê shĕʾôl, "ropes of Sheol") — the realm of the dead personified as an active snare-setter. מוֹקְשֵׁי מָוֶת (môqĕshê māwet, "snares of death") — môqēsh: a trap or snare for birds; death as a hunter laying traps. The four images of vv4-5 are parallel pairs: cords/ropes and floods/snares, building the picture of inescapable entrapment in mortal danger.
6: בַּצַּר-לִי אֶקְרָא יְהוָה (baṣṣar-lî ʾeqrāʾ YHWH, "In my distress I called to the LORD") — the same ṣar (tight place/distress) as Psalms 4:1; prayer from within the constriction. מֵהֵיכָלוֹ יִשְׁמַע קוֹלִי (mêhêkālô yishmaʿ qôlî, "From his temple he heard my voice") — the cry that rose from the tight earthly space is heard in the heavenly temple (hêkāl = palace/temple). שַׁוְעָתִי לְפָנָיו תָּבוֹא בְאָזְנָיו (shawyʿātî lĕpānāyw tābôʾ bĕʾāznāyw) — "my cry came before him into his ears": the two-stage movement (before his face, then into his ears) suggests deliberate hearing, not accidental reception.
7: The theophany begins. וַתִּגְעַשׁ וַתִּרְעַשׁ הָאָרֶץ (wattīgʿash wattirʿash hāʾāreṣ, "The earth shook and trembled") — two verbs for earth-movement: gāʿash (to quake, storm) and rāʿash (to shake, tremble). Both are used in theophanic contexts (Isaiah 24:19-20). כִּי-חָרָה לוֹ (kî-ḥārāh lô, "for he was angry") — literally "for it burned to him" — ḥārāh uses the burning metaphor for divine anger (cf. Psalms 2:5). The earth's convulsion is the response to divine wrath, not an independent seismic event.
8: עָלָה עָשָׁן בְּאַפּוֹ (ʿālāh ʿāshān bĕʾappô, "Smoke poured from his nostrils") — the anthropomorphism is striking: smoke rises from the divine nose/anger (ʾap = nostril/anger). The imagery draws on volcano imagery and on the Sinai theophany (Exodus 19:18: "Mount Sinai was covered with smoke, for the LORD descended on it in fire"). וְאֵשׁ מִפִּיו תֹּאכֵל (wĕʾêsh mippîw tōʾkel, "consuming fire from his mouth") — divine speech as fire. Gāḥālîm (coals/embers) blazing before him: the attendant of divine presence.
9: וַיֵּט שָׁמַיִם וַיֵּרַד (wayyêṭ shāmayim wayyêrad, "He bowed the heavens and came down") — the heavens as a fabric that can be bent down (nāṭāh: to stretch out, to incline). The divine descent through bent-down heaven is the theophanic idiom for God entering the human plane. וַעֲרָפֶל תַּחַת רַגְלָיו (waʿărāpel taḥat raglāyw, "dark cloud under his feet") — ʿărāpel is the thick, impenetrable darkness associated with divine presence at Sinai (Deuteronomy 4:11; 1 Kings 8:12). Cloud as the floor the divine warrior walks on.
10: וַיִּרְכַּב עַל-כְּרוּב וַיָּעֹף (wayyirkab ʿal-kĕrûb wayyāʿōp, "He mounted a cherub and flew") — the divine chariot imagery: God riding the cherub. The cherubim are the ark-attendants (Exodus 25:18-20) and the throne-bearers of the divine chariot (Ezekiel 1:5-21). יִדֶּה עַל-כַּנְפֵי-רוּחַ (yiddeh ʿal-kanpê-rûaḥ, "he soared on the wings of the wind") — the wind as the vehicle of divine movement, consistent with Psalms 104:3-4.
11: יָשֶׁת חֹשֶׁךְ סִתְרוֹ (yāshet ḥōshek sitrô, "He made darkness his shelter") — paradox: the God of light (Psalms 27:1; 1 John 1:5) conceals himself in darkness (ḥōshek). The divine warrior approaches unseen, in the cloud-darkness that protects his unapproachable holiness. סְכָּתוֹ סְבִיבוֹתָיו (sĕkātô sĕbîbôtāyw, "his canopy about him") — sukkāh as divine shelter: the same word for the booths of the Festival of Tabernacles.
12: מִנֹּגַהּ נֶגְדּוֹ (minnegah negdô, "From the brightness of his presence") — nōgah: brightness, radiance; even from within the dark cloud, the divine presence radiates outward. The clouds burst forth with בָּרָד וְגַחֲלֵי-אֵשׁ (bārād wĕgaḥălê-ʾêsh, "hailstones and coals of fire") — the storm weapons of the divine warrior, inverting the expected: the hidden dark cloud does not merely conceal but actively discharges judgment.
13: וְעֶלְיוֹן יִתֵּן קֹלוֹ (wĕʿelyôn yittên qōlô, "the Most High raised his voice") — thunder as divine speech. Qôl YHWH (voice of the LORD) is the thunder motif developed fully in Ps 29. The title ʿElyôn (Most High) is the Canaanite divine title absorbed into Israelite theology — used here alongside YHWH, declaring that the Most High God and the covenantal LORD are one.
14: וַיִּשְׁלַח חִצָּיו וַיְפִיצֵם (wayyishlah ḥiṣṣāyw wayyĕpîṣêm, "He shot his arrows and scattered them") — lightning as divine arrows. The imagery maps onto the Canaanite storm-god Baal who wielded lightning-weapons, but here repurposed entirely for YHWH's combat on behalf of his servant. The enemies scattered by divine lightning are not the cosmic enemies but David's historical opponents, showing that cosmic theophanic power is deployed for concrete human rescue.
15: וַיִּגָּלוּ אֲפִיקֵי מַיִם (wayyiggālû ʾăpîqê māyim, "The channels of the sea were exposed") — at the divine rebuke, the sea retreats and its underwater channels are laid bare. This echoes the Sea of Reeds: the OT consistently uses the Exodus sea-crossing as the type of divine warrior rescue. מִגַּעֲרָתְךָ יְהוָה (migaʿărātĕkā YHWH, "at your rebuke, O LORD") — gĕʿārāh (rebuke, scolding): the sea retreats not from force but from the divine word of rebuke — the same word used in Psalms 104:7 for the waters fleeing at creation.
16: יִשְׁלַח מִמָּרוֹם יִקָּחֵנִי (yishlah mimmārôm yiqqāḥênî, "He reached down from on high and took hold of me") — the rescue is initiated entirely from above. Mārom (height, the high place) is God's position; from there he sends and takes (lāqaḥ: to take, seize, receive). מִמַּיִם רַבִּים (mimmaym rabbîm, "from deep waters") — the many/great waters as the chaos-force from which rescue is made; the same waters exposed in v15 are the peril from which the Psalmist is drawn. The image mirrors the infant Moses drawn from the Nile (māshāh, Exodus 2:10 — "because I drew him out of the water").
17: מֵאֹיְבִי עָז (mêʾōyĕbî ʿāz, "from my powerful enemy") — ʿāz (strong, mighty): the enemy is not merely hostile but powerful, genuinely stronger than the Psalmist (v17b: "for they were stronger than I was"). The admission of inferiority makes the rescue more remarkable — not a story of triumph by superior David, but of weak David rescued by the omnipotent LORD.
18: יְקַדְּמוּנִי בְיוֹם-אֵידִי (yĕqaddĕmûnî bĕyôm-ʾêdî, "They confronted me in the day of my disaster") — qādham (piel): to meet/confront (with hostile intent). ʾÊd (calamity, disaster): the word for appointed doom/misfortune — the day when everything was against him. וַיְהִי יְהוָה לְמִשְׁעָן לִי (wayhî YHWH lĕmishʿān lî, "but the LORD was my support") — mishʿān: a staff to lean on, a prop. God as the physical support when the Psalmist could not stand on his own.
19: וַיֹּצִיאֵנִי לַמֶּרְחָב (wayyôṣîʾênî lammerhāb, "He brought me out into an open place") — the same spatial metaphor as Psalms 4:1 (hirchabātā, "you enlarged me"): salvation as movement from the tight place (ṣar) to wide-open space (merehāb). כִּי חָפֵץ בִּי (kî ḥāpēṣ bî, "because he was pleased with me") — the reason for rescue: divine delight/desire (ḥāpēṣ). Not David's merit but the divine pleasure as the ground of rescue — the same word used in Psalms 5:4 (God does not delight in wickedness) deployed in its positive form.
20: יִגְמְלֵנִי יְהוָה כְּצִדְקִי (yigmĕlênî YHWH kĕṣidqî, "The LORD rewarded me according to my righteousness") — the theme of righteous vindication: gāmal (to repay, to deal out according to). Ṣedeq (righteousness) and bōr yādāyw (cleanness of hands) are the covenantal integrity that David claims — not sinless perfection but covenant faithfulness. The specific righteousness claimed in vv20-24 is not absolute but relational: faithfulness to the covenant relationship.
21: שָׁמַרְתִּי דַּרְכֵי יְהוָה (shāmartî darkê YHWH, "I have kept to the LORD's ways") — shāmar: to keep, guard, observe (the same word used for the cherubim guarding the garden, Genesis 3:24, and for keeping the commandments). The claim is of faithfulness to the covenantal path, not of personal superiority. לֹא-רָשַׁעְתִּי מֵאֱלֹהָי (lōʾ-rāshaʿtî mêʾĕlōhāy, "I have not turned away from my God in rebellion") — rāshaʿ: to act wickedly, to be in the wrong. The negative formulation: no deliberate departure.
22: כִּי כָּל-מִשְׁפָּטָיו לְנֶגְדִּי (kî kol-mishpāṭāyw lĕnegdî, "All his rules were set before me") — mishpāṭ (judgment/rule/ordinance) as the standard placed before the eyes. Lĕnegdî: before me, in my sight — the image of the covenantal instruction as a constant visual reference. חֻקֹּתָיו לֹא-אָסִיר מֶנִּי (ḥuqqōtāyw lōʾ-ʾāsîr mennî, "I did not set aside his statutes") — ḥōq (statute/ordinance) in contrast to mishpāṭ: the fixed decrees vs the case-by-case judgments.
23: וָאֶהְיֶה תָמִים עִמּוֹ (wāʾehyeh tāmîm ʿimmô, "I was blameless before him") — tāmîm: unblemished, whole, without defect — the same word used for the sacrificial animal that must be without blemish (Exodus 12:5; Leviticus 1:3). The Psalmist claims to have been before God with the quality of a valid offering. וָאֶשְׁתַּמֵּר מֵעֲוֹנִי (wāʾeshtammêr mêʿăwōnî, "I kept myself from wrongdoing") — hithpael reflexive of shāmar: actively guarding oneself from one's own potential iniquity (ʿāwôn).
24: The repetition of v20's language: כְּצִדְקִי (kĕṣidqî, "according to my righteousness") and כְּבֹר יָדַי (kĕbōr yādāy, "according to the cleanness of my hands") in God's sight (לְנֶגֶד עֵינָיו). The claim of vv20-24 forms a unit bounded by these matching formulas, with the specific evidences of faithfulness in vv21-23 as the middle section.
25:
עִם-חָסִיד תִּתְחַסָּד (ʿim-ḥāsîd titḥassād, "To the faithful you show yourself faithful") — the divine mirror principle: each verb is a niphal or hithpael reflexive showing God "reflecting back" the character of the one approaching him. The pattern is: ḥāsîd (faithful/loyal one) → titḥassād (you act as the faithful/loyal one toward them). Three pairs follow:

v25: faithful/loyal (ḥāsîd → titḥassād) — v25b: blameless (tāmîm → tittammām) — v26a: pure (bārûr → tittabbār) — v26b: devious/crooked (ʿiqqêsh → tittappe.l, "you show yourself cunning/twisting"). The final pair is the reversal: with the crooked, God acts according to their crookedness — not by becoming unjust, but because justice to the unjust has the appearance of cunning from their perspective.
26: וְעִם-עִקֵּשׁ תִּתְפַּתָּל (wĕʿim-ʿiqqêsh tittapattāl, "but to the devious you show yourself cunning") — ʿiqqêsh: perverse, twisted (from ʿāqash, to twist). Tittapattāl (hithpael of pātal): to twist, wrestle, be twisted. The reciprocity is not that God becomes devious but that his consistency in pursuing justice "looks like" cunning or resistance to those who are crooked. The divine way is single; its appearance differs depending on the orientation of the one encountering it.
27: כִּי-אַתָּה עַם-עָנִי תוֹשִׁיעַ (kî-ʾattāh ʿam-ʿānî tôshîaʿ, "For you save an afflicted people") — ʿānî: poor, afflicted, humble — the recipient of divine salvation is specifically the one without resources or pretension. וְעֵינַיִם רָמוֹת תַּשְׁפִּיל (wĕʿênayim rāmôt tashpîl, "but proud eyes you humble") — rāmôt: raised, lifted up; shāpal (hiphil): to bring low. The reversal is consistent: the humble are lifted (v19: to the open place) and the proud are brought down.
28: כִּי-אַתָּה תָּאִיר נֵרִי (kî-ʾattāh tāʾîr nêrî, "you light my lamp") — nêr: lamp, light; ʾûr (hiphil: to cause to shine, to light). The lamp as the symbol of continuing life and divine favor (1 Kings 11:36: "I will give him one tribe, so that my servant David may always have a lamp before me in Jerusalem"). יְהוָה אֱלֹהַי יַגִּיהַּ חָשְׁכִּי (YHWH ʾĕlōhay yagîah ḥāshkî, "the LORD my God brightens my darkness") — nāgah (hiphil: to cause to shine). The darkness (danger, defeat, despair) becomes light through divine illumination — not the removal of the dark situation but the illumination within it.
29: כִּי בְךָ אָרֻץ גְּדוּד (kî bĕkā ʾāruṣ gĕdûd, "For by your help I can charge against a raiding party") — gĕdûd: a raiding band, a marauding troop. Rûṣ (to run, to charge). וּבֵאלֹהַי אֲדַלֶּג-שׁוּר (ûbêʾĕlōhay ʾădallēg-shûr, "by my God I can scale a wall") — dālag: to leap/spring; shûr: a wall or enemy stronghold. Both images contrast the Psalmist's own limitation with what becomes possible "in God" (bĕkā, bêʾĕlōhay) — the "in Christ" language of Paul has its OT template here.
30: הָאֵל תָּמִים דַּרְכּוֹ (hāʾêl tāmîm darkô, "This God — his way is perfect") — tāmîm (perfect/unblemished) applied now to God's way rather than David's conduct (v23). אִמְרַת יְהוָה צְרוּפָה (ʾimrat YHWH ṣĕrûpāh, "the LORD's word is refined and true") — ṣārap: to smelt, refine (as gold or silver is refined in fire to remove impurities). The divine word has undergone the refinement process — it is pure without alloy. Psalms 12:6 uses the same image for the divine promises.
31: כִּי מִי אֱלוֹהַ מִבַּלְעֲדֵי יְהוָה (kî mî ʾĕlôah mibbaleʿădê YHWH, "For who is God besides the LORD?") — the rhetorical question presupposes monolatry at minimum: no other claimant to divinity has the qualities just described. מִי צוּר זוּלָתִי אֱלֹהֵינוּ (mî ṣûr zûlātî ʾĕlōhênû, "who is a rock except our God?") — ṣûr (rock as refuge and fortress): the test of a divine claim is whether the one claiming divinity can actually provide the stability and protection the rock-metaphor requires. The exclusive answer: only YHWH.
32: הָאֵל הַמְאַזְּרֵנִי חָיִל (hāʾêl hamĕʾazzĕrênî ḥāyil, "It is God who arms me with strength") — ʾāzar (piel: to gird, to clothe with a belt/girdle): the image of equipping for battle by fastening the belt/sash that holds the weapons. Ḥāyil: strength, valor, military might. God as the one who does the arming — the passive recipient of divine equipment. וַיִּתֵּן תָּמִים דַּרְכִּי (wayyittên tāmîm darkî, "and makes my way perfect") — tāmîm applied to the Psalmist's path: God completes or perfects what was incomplete.
33: מְשַׁוֶּה רַגְלַי כָּאַיָּלוֹת (mĕshawweh raglāy kāʾayyālôt, "he makes my feet like the feet of a deer") — shāwāh (piel: to make equal, to level, to set properly); ʾayyālāh (doe, hind — the nimble mountain deer). The feet of the deer that navigate rocky terrain without slipping are the image for divinely-given sure-footedness in difficult circumstances. עַל בָּמֹתַי יַעֲמִידֵנִי (ʿal bāmōtay yaʿămîdênî, "sets me securely on the heights") — bāmāh: high place; to stand on the heights is military advantage — commanding ground.
34: מְלַמֵּד יָדַי לַמִּלְחָמָה (mĕlammêd yādāy lammilḥāmāh, "he trains my hands for battle") — lāmad (piel: to teach, train — the same root as talmîd, disciple): God as the military trainer who teaches the hands their craft. וְנִחֲתָה קֶשֶׁת-נְחוּשָׁה זְרֹעֹתָי (wĕniḥătāh qeshet-nĕḥûshāh zĕrōʿōtāy, "my arms can bend a bow of bronze") — the bronze bow would normally exceed human strength; divine-given capacity enables what is naturally impossible.
35: וַתִּתֶּן-לִי מָגֵן יִשְׁעֶךָ (wattitten-lî māgên yishʿekā, "You have given me the shield of your salvation") — the shield is specifically "of your salvation" (yeshûʿâ): protective, yes, but the protection is the product of divine saving activity. וַעֲנָוַתְךָ תַרְבֵּנִי (waʿănāwātĕkā tarbênî, "your gentleness made me great") — the key term ʿănāwāh: condescension, humility, gentleness. It is the LORD's condescension (ʿānāw: humble/meek) toward the Psalmist that makes him great. This is the paradox at the heart of the psalm: greatness comes not from David's power but from divine condescension that stoops to lift.
36: תַּרְחִיב צַעֲדִי תַחְתָּי (tarḥîb ṣaʿădî taḥtāy, "You gave wide room for my steps beneath me") — rāḥab (hiphil: to widen/enlarge): the spatial metaphor of salvation as room to move, returning from Psalms 4:1 and Psalms 18:19. Ṣaʿad (steps, stride) — a wide stride, confident movement. וְלֹא מָעֲדוּ קַרְסֻלָּי (wĕlōʾ māʿădû qarsullāy, "my feet did not slip") — māʿad: to slip, to totter; qarsôl: ankle. The ankle as the vulnerable joint of confident movement: divine enlargement of the path prevented the ankle-turning that a tight path would cause.
37: אֶרְדֹּף אוֹיְבַי וְאַשִּׂיגֵם (ʾerdōp ʾôyĕbay wĕʾassîgêm, "I pursued my enemies and overtook them") — the pursuit language reverses vv4-5: David was the hunted one, now he pursues. Rādap (to pursue, chase) and nāśag (to overtake, reach): the decisive engagement. וְלֹא-אָשׁוּב עַד-כַּלּוֹתָם (wĕlōʾ-āshûv ʿad-kallôtām, "and did not turn back until they were finished") — kālāh (to complete/finish/destroy): no premature withdrawal; the pursuit continues to completion.
38: אֶמְחָצֵם וְלֹא-יֻכְלוּ קוּם (ʾemḥāṣêm wĕlōʾ-yûklû qûm, "I struck them down so that they could not rise") — māḥaṣ: to shatter, to smite decisively. The inability to rise after being struck is the language of permanent defeat. יִפְּלוּ תַּחַת רַגְלָי (yippĕlû taḥat raglāy, "they fell under my feet") — the posture of complete submission: under the feet, the position of the vanquished before the victor (cf. Psalms 110:1; Joshua 10:24).
39: וַתְּאַזְּרֵנִי חַיִל לַמִּלְחָמָה (watĕʾazzĕrênî ḥayil lammilḥāmāh, "For you armed me with strength for the battle") — resuming the divine-equipping motif of v32; the battle's outcome is not David's achievement but the product of divine arming. תַּכְרִיעַ קָמַי תַּחְתָּי (takrîaʿ qāmay taḥtāy, "you brought down under me those who rose against me") — kāraʿ (hiphil: to cause to kneel/bow down): God as the one who brings the adversaries to their knees beneath David.
40: וְאֹיְבַי נָתַתָּה לִּי עֹרֶף (wĕʾōyĕbay nātattāh llî ʿōrep, "You made my enemies turn their backs in retreat") — ʿōrep (back of the neck): to show the back of the neck is to flee in retreat; the enemies are turned to expose their most vulnerable position. וּמְשַׂנְאַי אַצְמִיתֵם (ûmĕsanʾay ʾaṣmîtêm, "I destroyed those who hated me") — ṣāmat: to silence, destroy, cut off (rare word; the hiphil indicates causing to be silenced).
41: יְשַׁוְּעוּ וְאֵין-מוֹשִׁיעַ (yĕshawwĕʿû wĕʾên-môshîaʿ, "They cried for help, but there was no one to save them") — the enemies' cry reverses David's: he cried (v6) and was heard; they cry and find no môshîaʿ (savior/deliverer). עַל-יְהוָה וְלֹא עָנָם (ʿal-YHWH wĕlōʾ ʿānām, "they even cried to the LORD, but he did not answer them") — the contrast is sharp: the same LORD who heard David's cry (v6) did not answer their cry. Not arbitrary: they were the enemies of his anointed and had no relational claim on his response.
42: וְאֶשְׁחָקֵם כְּעָפָר עַל-פְּנֵי-רוּחַ (wĕʾeshḥāqêm kĕʿāpār ʿal-pĕnê-rûaḥ, "I ground them fine as windblown dust") — shāḥaq: to grind, to pulverize (the same root as fine dust). Kĕʿāpār ʿal-pĕnê-rûaḥ: "like dust on the face of the wind." The contrast with v3 (chaff blown by the wind in Ps 1) is instructive: the enemies become the chaff, scattered by the wind of divine judgment.
43: תְּפַלְּטֵנִי מֵרִיבֵי עָם (tĕpalĕṭênî mêrîbê ʿām, "You saved me from conflicts with my own people") — rîb: legal dispute, quarrel; the enemies within the people, not only foreign nations. תְּשִׂימֵנִי לְרֹאשׁ גּוֹיִם (tĕsîmênî lĕrōʾsh gôyim, "you made me head of the nations") — the scope of David's kingship extends to the nations; the language anticipates the royal universalism of Psalms 2:8. Paul cites v49 in this context as the ground for Gentile worship.
44: לִשְׁמוֹעַ אֹזֶן יִשָּׁמְעוּ-לִי (lishmoaʿ ʾōzen yishshāmĕʿû-lî, "As soon as they heard of me, they obeyed") — literally "at the hearing of the ear they obeyed me." Shāmaʿ appears three times in this phrase, emphasizing the immediacy and totality of the submission. בְּנֵי-נֵכָר יְכַחֲשׁוּ-לִי (bĕnê-nêkār yĕkaḥăshû-lî, "foreigners submitted to me") — kāḥash: to fail, deny, cringe/submit reluctantly; the foreigners' submission is not voluntary homage but the submission of the defeated.
45: בְּנֵי-נֵכָר יִבֹּלוּ (bĕnê-nêkār yibbōlû, "Foreigners lost their courage") — nābēl: to wither, to fade (as a leaf withers); the courage of the foreign peoples wilted. וְיַחְגְּרוּ מִמִּסְגְּרוֹתָם (wĕyaḥgĕrû mimmisĕgrôtām, "came out of their fortresses trembling") — ḥāgar: to gird oneself (for movement or battle); misegĕrôt: strongholds, locked places. The irony: they gird themselves to emerge from their fortresses — not in bold battle-readiness but in trembling, forced submission.
46: חַי-יְהוָה וּבָרוּךְ צוּרִי (ḥay-YHWH ûbārûk ṣûrî, "The LORD lives! Blessed be my Rock!") — ḥay YHWH: "As the LORD lives!" — an oath formula certifying that what follows is true. But here it also functions as a declaration of divine life in contrast to the dead idols who cannot hear or respond (contrast v41). Bārûk ṣûrî: formal blessing directed toward God, now using bārûk (the formal blessing-word) rather than ʾashrê. וְיָרוּם אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׁעִי (wĕyārûm ʾĕlōhê yishʿî, "Exalted be the God of my salvation") — a wish or declaration: may the God of my salvation be lifted high.
47: הָאֵל הַנֹּתֵן נְקָמוֹת לִי (hāʾêl hannōtên nĕqāmôt lî, "the God who gives me justice") — nĕqāmāh: vengeance, vindication; the MKT renders it "justice" to capture the sense of setting right what was wrong. וַיַּדְבֵּר עַמִּים תַּחְתָּי (wayyaddêr ʿammîm taḥtāy, "subdues peoples under me") — dābar (hiphil?): actually this should be wĕyaddêr from dābar meaning to subdue/bring low; the peoples placed under him.
48: מְפַלְּטִי מֵאֹיְבָי (mĕpallĕṭî mêʾōyĕbāy, "who rescued me from my enemies") — the same root pālaṭ from v2 (mĕphalṭî): rescuer. The psalm circles back to the opening cluster of rescue metaphors. אַף מִן-קָמַי תְּרוֹמְמֵנִי (ʾap min-qāmay tĕrômĕmênî, "you lifted me above those who rose against me") — rûm (hiphil: to lift up, exalt): the divine lifting is above those who tried to bring down.
49: עַל-כֵּן אוֹדְךָ בַגּוֹיִם יְהוָה (ʿal-kên ʾôdĕkā baggôyim YHWH, "Therefore I will give you thanks, O LORD, among the nations") — the climactic thanksgiving. בַגּוֹיִם (baggôyim): "among the nations/Gentiles" — the Davidic king's testimony extends beyond Israel to the watching Gentile nations. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 15:9 as a proof text that the Scriptures anticipated Gentile worship: "I will praise you among the Gentiles." The Psalm's historical statement (David's international testimony) becomes the NT's foundation for Gentile inclusion in the praise of God.
50: מַגְדִּיל יְשׁוּעוֹת מַלְכּוֹ (magdîl yĕshûʿôt malkô, "He gives great salvation to his king") — gādal (hiphil participle: causing to be great, magnifying): ongoing activity of magnifying salvations. The plural yĕshûʿôt (salvations) suggests multiple repeated rescues. וְעֹשֶׂה-חֶסֶד לִמְשִׁיחוֹ לְדָוִד וּלְזַרְעוֹ עַד-עוֹלָם (wĕʿōseh-ḥesed limshîḥô lĕdāwid ûlĕzarʿô ʿad-ʿôlām, "shows steadfast love to his anointed — to David and his descendants forever") — the ḥesed (covenant loyalty) promised to the māshîaḥ (anointed) extends forever (ʿad-ʿôlām). The psalm closes on the covenant: the Davidic line as the recipient of perpetual divine ḥesed, a promise that points beyond historical David to the eternal Anointed One.
